blocks.content: They sorted through the house slowly, a room at a time. There were things Kit kept — a watch, a recipe box in his mother's handwriting, a photograph of himself at nine years old.
He held the photograph for a long time.

Daisy boxed the kitchen. She didn't make it a task — she moved slowly, held things, set some aside. In the back of a drawer she found a clipping from the local paper, a small notice about Millhaven's documentary win at Austin. Ruth had cut it out. She'd had it in her kitchen drawer.
Daisy put it in the box she was keeping for Kit. Didn't say anything about it.
On their fourth night, after the work was done and the house was quiet, she told him she needed some air. He asked if she wanted company.

They walked without planning a direction. Millhaven after dark had its own grammar — the yellow porch lights, the sound of somewhere someone was watching television, the smell of the night in Georgia in April.
She knew where her feet were taking her before she said it.
